source.title: Predictive Place-Cell Sequences for Goal-Finding Emerge from Goal Memory and the Cognitive Map: A Computational Model

confidence_rationale: The paper provides a direct computational existence proof that injecting goal context (via prefrontal working memory signals) into hippocampal CA3 dynamics biases predictive place-cell sequences toward goal-relevant trajectories -- exactly the functional claim MECH-128 makes for E1. The mapping is high-fidelity at the functional level: goal memory modulates the recurrent attractor network and reshapes trajectory generation. Transfer risk is moderate because the substrate is rate-coded hippocampal CA3, not an LSTM; the gating mechanism (cortical attractor modulation via reward-potentiated synapses) differs architecturally from LSTM hidden-state concatenation.
failure_signatures: The model uses a continuous attractor network (CA3), not an LSTM; transfer to LSTM hidden-state concatenation requires an additional architectural assumption.
Goal conditioning is mediated by Hebbian/reward-potentiated cortico-hippocampal synapses, not by direct concatenation of a goal latent to the recurrent cell -- the REE mechanism is more direct.
Sequences emerge during stationary periods (analogous to replay/planning offline) and may not generalise to online E1 prediction at each recurrent step.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Injecting goal memory signals (cortical context representations) into hippocampal CA3 continuous-attractor dynamics produces predictive place-cell sequences that are systematically biased toward learned goal locations, without requiring virtual movement signals.
mapping.ree_translation: E1's LSTM hidden state plays the role of the CA3 recurrent network in this model. Providing z_goal_latent to E1 at each recurrent step is the LSTM analogue of the goal-memory input that biases CA3 attractor dynamics. Both mechanisms produce the same functional outcome: predictive trajectories are no longer goal-agnostic -- they are shaped by where the system is trying to go.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The biological substrate (continuous attractor, rate-coded CA3) is architecturally different from an LSTM. The biological goal signal is learned implicitly through reward-potentiated synapses, whereas MECH-128 proposes explicit concatenation or gating of z_goal_latent. The computational principle (goal context biases recurrent dynamics toward goal-relevant trajectories) is conserved, but the implementation pathway is not directly transferable.
mapping.source_context: Computational model of rat hippocampal navigation; place cells; continuous attractor network; spatial goal-finding task

confidence_components.notes: Frontiers journal, computational modeling, peer-reviewed. Strong functional mapping -- the paper is essentially a computational demonstration of MECH-128's core claim. Transfer risk comes from CA3 vs LSTM substrate difference and from online vs offline sequence generation.
